specification.topic-statement: The talloc wrapper seam exposes hierarchical memory allocation operations as individually mockable link seams so that tests can intercept or replace any allocation call without modifying production code.

scope.in-scope: Five allocation wrappers: zero-initialized allocation by size, string duplication, zero-initialized array allocation, reallocation, and formatted-string allocation.
scope.boundary: All allocations are performed within the hierarchical memory manager. The caller supplies a parent context for each operation; ownership of the resulting memory belongs to that context tree.

Tests define strong-symbol replacements for any subset of these wrappers. The strong definition shadows the default weak symbol, giving the test full control over that operation's behavior (e.g., simulating allocation failure by returning NULL).

behavior.build-mode-selection:
The wrappers exist in two forms depending on the build configuration.
In a debug or test build (when the NDEBUG macro is not defined), each wrapper is compiled as a weak symbol. A weak symbol has a default implementation but yields to any strong-symbol definition of the same name in the final link. This is the mechanism that makes individual wrappers replaceable in tests.
In a release build (when NDEBUG is defined), each wrapper is a static inline function defined in the header. No separate compilation unit is involved; the call is inlined at every call site with zero runtime overhead and no exported symbol.
The debug-build compilation unit that contains all five wrapper bodies is marked to be excluded from line-coverage measurement. This is intentional: the wrappers are pass-through delegators, and measuring coverage of one-line forwarding functions adds noise without signal.
behavior.zero-allocation: Allocates a block of the requested byte size as a child of the given parent context. The entire block is zero-initialized before being returned. Returns NULL if the allocation fails.
behavior.string-duplication: Allocates a null-terminated copy of the given string as a child of the given parent context. The length of the allocation equals the string length plus the null terminator. Returns NULL if the string pointer is NULL or if the allocation fails.
behavior.array-allocation:
Allocates a contiguous block sized to hold the given number of elements, each of the given byte size, as a child of the given parent context. The total byte count is the product of element size and element count. The entire block is zero-initialized before being returned.
Unlike a type-safe array macro, this wrapper accepts raw element size and count integers. The caller is responsible for passing the correct element size. The implementation multiplies the two values and calls the raw sized allocator — no overflow protection is applied to the multiplication.
behavior.reallocation:
Resizes an existing allocation to the given byte size, keeping the allocation under the given parent context. The parent context must match the context under which the pointer was originally allocated. Returns the (potentially moved) pointer, or NULL if the reallocation fails. If NULL is returned, the original allocation is freed.
The underlying memory manager does not require the parent context to perform a reallocation — it tracks ownership internally. The context parameter is present solely to satisfy the link-seam calling convention and to allow tests that inject failures based on the context.
behavior.formatted-string-allocation: Produces a formatted string as a child of the given parent context, using the given format string and variadic arguments. The behavior matches standard printf formatting. The variadic argument list is captured into a local argument list object, which is then forwarded to the underlying variadic-accepting allocator. The local argument list is cleaned up before the function returns. Returns the allocated string, or NULL if the allocation fails.
behavior.test-override-pattern:
A test that needs to control a specific allocation operation defines a function with the exact same signature as the wrapper, without any weak attribute. Because the linker resolves strong symbols before weak ones, the test's definition becomes the active implementation for that link unit. The test can then consult thread-local or file-local flags to decide whether to forward the call normally, return NULL to simulate out-of-memory, or return a controlled substitute value.
Thread-local state is used for the flags so that test binaries running multiple test suites in parallel do not interfere with one another.
